speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. And we're with Esther Rudomin Hautzig. Can you just tell me briefly about your-- when you came to the United States, your education, and what kind of work you did after?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the United States in May, 1947. I went to live with my uncle and aunt, Dorothy and Benjamin Hillson, in Brooklyn. I went to the Boro Hall Academy in Brooklyn in the summer to learn English, which I didn't know when I arrived. Then I went to the James Madison High School in Brooklyn after my summer class. And the principal there, a wonderful man named Dr. Max Neufeld-- N-E-U-F-E-L-D-- took me from department to department to see where I fit. And I was put in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] second half of the junior year by American educational counting. And I finished high school in two semesters and went to Hunter College, where I wanted to major in education after two years. But I was told that I could not teach in elementary school because I had an accent. So I quit college, married Walter Hautzig, who is a concert pianist, whom I met on the boat coming to America. And after getting married and traveling with Walter while he played concerts, I got a job in publishing in children's books, and have work in children's book publishing since 19-- January, 1951 until 1990. At the same time I wrote-- by 1990 I had written 17 books for children. One of them, Another one called And the reason I mention these books is not for publicity or ego's sake, but to-- to make it clear that your childhood never leaves you. as I said before, has been in print now for 27 years. I wrote a book called which is about my childhood in Vilna. I wrote which is stories about people in my life in Vilna and after.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wrote a book recently now, my 18th book, called which is about the Gaon in Vilna and a man named Samuel and a woman named Chaja-Rivka. And my father said, how can you write about me in the time of the Gaon? He lived in 17-something. 1730.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, Papa, it's a-- it's a story. Yeah, but you used my name and my wife's name. I said, your wife is my mother. I just used your names in the story. Anyway, I have now written 18 books. I have done the introduction to a new edition of which was published by the Jewish Publication Society in 1992. A facsimile edition of a history of Vilna written in 1943-- published in 1943 and written by a wonderful British historian, in Israel Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so everything I do has something to do with my childhood. I have done seven craft books. Why? Because I was taught crafts. Because I knitted in Siberia. Because I made curtains dyed with onion peels in Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote a book called Books about how to make presents out of nothing. And now I am just writing. I'm not working in publishing at the same time as writing. And I'm working on a book of stories and another story about my childhood in Vilna. And doing volunteer work in the library, which I said before. And with children through the New York City School Volunteer program in the South Bronx and in Brooklyn, wherever I'm assigned. Every year, I go to a different school. And we work on the children's writing and on storytelling. And I tell them how important it is for them to write stories about their childhoods and keep journals. How important it is to read and to write. And that's my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you just a few more questions. Is there or are there any events that stand out at the tippy top of your mind, more than anything else, from your years before the war, during the war, or immediately following?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I had one big crisis which I wrote about in I refused to make a present for my mother. The girl was-- in the story is named Sarah. It was me and it was actually about me, although I changed the story to suit my pleasure rather than the facts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's very dumb. And an editor once asked me, why don't you write about life before the war, not just during the war? And I said, nothing important happened. And I told her just this.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, well, write about that. And this book is then-- it's very well liked by children ages seven to 10. During the war, the thing that I remember most, very most, is being lost in the storm. And that my mother called the Shema, not just my name. And after the war, the year immediately after the war, I learned that you can't escape evil. Heniek died after surviving the war. And the evil follow him. Now, the most important things in my life are my family and my memories. I didn't think of that before. You just asked me this question. I live in the present, but I live as much in the past as I do in the present. If I want to-- when I'm silent-- and I love silence. I crave silence. That's what I loved about Siberia, the silence, the space, the quietness. I think more about what was than I do of what is. And I don't know that that is good, but that's the way it is. Maybe it's because I tell stories and I write books about what was and what is, what may be. I'm centered on stories. I like to tell them. I like to hear them. I write them. And you can't write stories without memories and without retaining what you hear. And something can be a memory a year later. It doesn't have to be 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I live-- it's like I put one of your tapes in my head and it keeps replaying and replaying and replaying. And I play it at different speeds and in different configurations. And that's the center. My family is my center. But this is the center of my inner life and my work. Did I answer your question? Yes. Yes. And I have one or two more. What would you say enabled you to cope through all these difficult circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll read you a letter from a child which answers this much better than anything I could possibly tell you. This came from a sixth grader on May 2, 1991. It was written on my father's-- it would have been my father's 92nd birthday. This girl wrote, 'My name is Kristin.' I'm not going to give the whole name. 'I'm 12 and your book means so much to me.' This is 'I have never seen or heard you speak before, so it's amazing how close I seem to be to you when I read some jotted down words on a piece of paper. But what you wrote, those weren't just words. They were feelings and hardships and love.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been so hard for you. I'll bet everyone says that. But you're so special to have had a family like yours. Do you ever wonder what it would be like if you were never shipped off to Siberia? I wonder how different your life would've been. It definitely wouldn't be as exciting. I do feel sorry for you. But do you need another person to pity you? I also think that you were lucky.'

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the answer to your question. 'Lucky to have parents. Lucky to have friends. Lucky to have food and a home. I will be glad for you rather than sorry, and think of the good times you had and savor them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the strongest and most courageous girl I have ever heard or knew or read of. You don't know how many times I've been the grocery store or looking around in my room and thought, what if Esther had this? It must have been hard for you to write this book, but I thank you for doing it.'

speaker: interviewee
text: The central paragraph is that I was really lucky to be with my parents and to have a roof over my head, which is a home [? in her. ?] And to have had friends and to have had love. And that is what it's all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: My final question is are there any words of wisdom you would like to leave us with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the final words of wisdom were in Kristin's paragraph. That as long as you have love and family and a roof over your head and food, you're lucky. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The very final word to it is never to give up. Never to give up. Everyone has a Siberia. Just don't let the snow get you. Keep going. So many people tried and couldn't make it. I am one of the lucky ones who succeeded in getting through a terrible time and being able to talk about it. And I just hope this is not just for today and tomorrow, these tapes. These are for a long time to come in. And you don't know what history brings. Just as long as we all know that people are stronger than what they think they have in them, and the strength comes up at moments when you least expect it. And as long as you know it's there, use it. Use it. And never give up. Never give up if it is within your power. A gun to your head isn't going to-- to make you live if a Nazi wants to shoot you. But a difficult period can be overcome. Siberia was not Nazi Germany, and I overcame it. And there will be Siberias in everyone's life, whether it's losing a member of the family, or losing a parent, or worse. We can all-- not all-- many of us can overcome the worst obstacles. I don't know that these are words of wisdom. They're just experiences from a fairly long life lived in all kinds of circumstances. So I thank you for this opportunity to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Well, this is my husband, Walter Hautzig. And we met on the boat coming to America, but he wasn't an immigrant. He was coming from his first tour to Europe as a concert pianist in 1947.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to be grateful to the king of Norway. Esther never met the king of Norway, but if I hadn't met the king of Norway, I would never have met Esther. I gave a concert for the benefit of the Nazi victims in Oslo, and as a result, I had an audience with the king. And because of that, I had to change from one boat to another. And on the boat that I eventually took was this 16-year-old girl with braids who didn't speak English, and she's been my wife for 44 years and she corrects all my spelling mistakes now. And I'm very happy I took that boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Deborah Margolee Hautzig. Her middle name is after my Aunt Margola, about whom we spoke. And it had caused some tough times for Debbie, as we all call her. I think it was very hard for her to be the child of Holocaust survivors, but that's my view of it. And she's just the best friend anyone can have after some hard times over the years. I can't imagine having more fun in this whole entire world talking to anyone than to Debbie. For one thing, we are in the same family. And for another, we are in the same field. She's a wonderful writer. A wonderful writer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I agree.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah! The best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Debbie, one question for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you to be raised by Holocaust survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I could answer that in about 25 years, if you-- but it's much harder to be unhappy when your parents have been through the Holocaust, or to express it because you feel like nothing that you go through could begin to compare to what they went through, and you feel guilty even saying it. I-- you didn't tell about the lamb chop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I did. I most assuredly did.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hard in some ways because I-- I never felt like the things that hurt me counted compared to-- and also, I didn't want to make them unhappy. I wanted to make them feel like they did absolutely everything right all the time. And so even when I was upset about something, I wouldn't tell them because I didn't want them to think less of themselves, not to mention less of me, because I thought less of myself. I felt it was my failure. So, you know, for starters. But I wouldn't have it any other way. They're the best. I love them. I adore them. I would die-- I would do anything for them. They are funny and smart, and they've given me an amazing life that I wouldn't trade for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother, whose name was Chaja-Rivka [INAUDIBLE]. Everyone called her [? Ria. ?] Her married name was [? Ria ?] Rudomin. And the child is me. And it was done in a photographer's studio in Vilna, Poland, in 1936, I would say, '35 or '36, when I was either five or six years old. The picture is of my grandfather, my father's father, Shimon. No. Shlomo Mordecai Ben Shimon Rudomin. I sit on his lap. And next to him to the right as we face the picture is the daughter of a cousin of my mother's whose name was Nusia Shimiensky. It is in a-- in a place near Vilna called either [? Niemcza ?] or [? Valakampiai. ?] I'm afraid I don't remember whether-- I think it was in [? Valakampiai, ?] which was a summer place about an hour away from Vilna. And this is in the summer of '36 or '37. This is a picture of Miss Rachel, my nanny. Rokhelle [INAUDIBLE], now Rokhelle Pollack. I sit on her lap. And below both of us is my cousin, Musik, who died all alone in the Vilna ghetto before he reached his bar mitzvah. And nobody knows how he died. My cousin-- my mother's cousin Danka saw him roaming the streets. We don't know when he died and how he died, and whether he was alone or with someone. And his future was limitless. He could've been a social worker or a lawyer. He always settled our disputes. He was fair and kind and loving. And his life was taken before he was 13, in 1943 or '42. No one knows. This is a picture on the beach in [? Valakampiai. ?] Of that, I'm sure. Of four good friends from Vilna. This first one is Dina Bromowitz. The second one is--

speaker: interviewer
text: First one from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Second-- first one from-- sorry-- on the left is Dina Bromowitz. The second one's name is Bronia, and her last name I do not know. The next one is a woman named Slava. I do not know her last name. The first one on the right and the last one in the group is my Aunt Margola, whose name I've given to Debbie, my daughter, who went with her mother to the left when she could've gone to the right. And the name that I gave Debbie for Margola has caused her difficulties because for some reason, she took it into her head that it was an implication that if I was in danger, she would have to be the one to go to the left with me and save me. And it wasn't until about three or four years ago when she called me up and she said, Ma, I finally know why you gave me Margola's name. Because I wrote about her. You named me for Margola not for the way she died, but for the way she lived. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of [INAUDIBLE], my mother's brother, Elle Chim. Which one? Starting from which direction?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will say it. The first one on the left with the fedora. I found this picture several months after I went to Vilna looking for his picture in the drawer of someone's living room desk. The next person to [INAUDIBLE] is a cousin of my mother's whose name is-- was Sasha Mysowiesky. He lived in Warsaw. The next person to him, the pretty woman with the hat, is Musia Levin, the daughter of my mother's father's sister Rifka Levin and Lippa Levin of Vilna. Next to them, the last one in the homburg, is named Shlamik Gabrielev, who was a relative of my mother's who lived in Warsaw. In back of them is the castle hill that I mentioned where we children went to play. The year is '38, I would say. '37 or '38 in Vilna. This is a picture in Vilna in 1935, I would say. The first person on the left is Musik in his beautiful cap. Next to him is his sister, Sona, who was killed the first months of occupation by the Nazis in Vilna. She and her mother were killed in [INAUDIBLE] after having dug their own graves. Next to her is Miss Rachel, who I mentioned before, my governess. And next to her is myself with Japanese umbrella on one of the main streets in Vilna. This is a picture of my great Uncle Max Hilson, whose name in Vilna was Moses [? Cunzer. ?] He was my mother's father's oldest brother. Next to him sits my great-grandmother Raza [? Cunzer, ?] who was about 85 or 84 in this picture, in '37, when her son came to visit from America. Remarkable thing about Bubbe Raza was that she decided in the ghetto in Vilna in 1941 that the Germans are not going to have the pleasure of killing her as they were killing others. She was going to die of her own free will. She went to bed. She prepared her burial clothes, called [YIDDISH]. She had a candle to put by her head. And that evening, she died. She also had a Torah written in her house in the 1920s, which another son of hers named Yankel Cunzer brought to the Society for the Advancement of Judaism on the West 86th Street in New York, and it still stands in the [INAUDIBLE]. And I consider that woman a miracle, in the way she lived and in the way she died, and the legacy she left. This is a photograph of me in Rubtsovsk, Siberia, on the marketplace. You can see in back the logs of a house which was on the edge of the marketplace. It is an enlargement of a postage sized ID, the only picture I have myself during my years in Siberia. And I must have been 12 years older around them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wore the same sweater which my mother packed in Vilna, which I wore to go back to Poland. And my daughter says, Ma, you had class in Siberia, too. And I said, how is that? And she said, look at that hanky in your breast pocket. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very funny comment on a picture of someone who was deported to Siberia. Class doesn't exactly go with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's brother, Dovid Rudomin. His full name was Dovid Ben Shlomo Rudomin. And this is a picture of him, we think, which my grandmother and I saw in a window of a photographer's studio in Lodz in 1946. And my grandmother and I both shrieked that this is Uncle [INAUDIBLE], who died during the Holocaust. Having passed as a Christian, he was blond and blue-eyed and extremely Gentile-looking. Looked like a Gentile. And he was on the run for over a year and a half, passing as a German, even to the extent that he worked in the city hall of a small town in Poland. He spoke perfect German and Polish. And then he came to a small town in Warsaw and someone recognized him and reported him to the SS, and he was shot. The reason I know this story in such detail is that a man named Nehemia Glezer and his wife, Ida Glezer-- G-L-E-Z-E-R, were also on the run, and their paths crossed. The picture included two other men on either side of my Uncle [INAUDIBLE]. And my grandmother insisted that she wanted to have a copy just of the person she was convinced was her son. And the photographer in Lodz couldn't tell us where he got the picture, who these people were. It was in a box of photographs that he found in the shop after the war. We think it isn't [INAUDIBLE]. I want to think it's [INAUDIBLE]. It's a wonderful likeness of what [INAUDIBLE] looked like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Samuel Rudomin, in Sweden after the war, 1947, in a suburb of Stockholm called [INAUDIBLE], standing by the house which we rented. And he looks just as cheerful and just as young in '47 as he did in '37 and as he did into his 90th year in New York.
